lists.title: Dutch A1 Grammar Path
lists.description: First Dutch: verb-second order, de/het, pronouns with polite u, zijn and hebben, the three-form present, plurals, niet/geen, questions, the adjective -e rule, possessives, and er is/er zijn.

lists.title: Dutch A2 Grammar Path
lists.description: The working past and more: the perfect with 't kofschip, the imperfect, modals, separable verbs, verb-final subclauses, futures, comparisons, diminutives, om te, and the imperative.

lists.title: Dutch B1 Grammar Path
lists.description: Fluent plumbing: the five jobs of er, relative die/dat/wie, reflexives, the worden/zijn passive, want vs omdat, te-infinitives, zou, and indirect speech.

lists.title: Dutch B2 Grammar Path
lists.description: Sounding Dutch: modal particles, the past perfect, double infinitives, aan het + zitten te progressives, waar-compounds, and conditionals.

lists.title: Dutch C1 Grammar Path
lists.description: Polish: register and letter-craft, nominalization, the sentence bracket (tang), and posture verbs as 'to be'.

lists.title: Dutch C2 Grammar Path
lists.description: Mastery: Netherlands vs Flanders, and the case-system fossils that still walk the streets.

points.title: Word order: the verb comes second
points.function: Build your first sentences — one verb slot, position two
points.explanation: A Dutch main clause puts the conjugated verb in SECOND position, whatever comes first: Ik drink koffie (I drink coffee), Morgen drink ik koffie (tomorrow drink I coffee — the subject hops behind the verb!). Starting with time or place is completely normal; forgetting to flip the subject is the classic beginner error.

drills.translation: Tomorrow I am going to Amsterdam.
drills.hint: to go — verb stays second, subject flips
to stay — after a time word
to understand — subject after the verb

points.title: De and het: the two genders
points.function: Every noun is a de-word or a het-word
points.explanation: Dutch has two articles: de (common gender — about 75% of nouns, and ALL plurals) and het (neuter — including every diminutive on -je). Een is 'a/an' for both. There is no reliable rule: learn each noun with its article. Het-words matter later — they change adjective endings and pronouns.

drills.translation: The man walks in the street.

points.title: Subject pronouns: ik, jij, u, hij, zij…
points.function: Say who does what — with the polite u
points.explanation: ik (I), jij/je (you — informal), u (you — polite, singular AND plural), hij (he), zij/ze (she), het (it), wij/we (we), jullie (you all), zij/ze (they). The full forms (jij, zij, wij) add emphasis; the short ones (je, ze, we) are the everyday default. u takes the same verb form as jij.

points.title: Zijn — present (ben, bent, is, zijn)
points.function: Say who or what someone is
points.explanation: zijn (to be): ik ben, jij bent (but: ben jij? — the -t drops when jij follows!), u bent, hij/zij/het is, wij/jullie/zij zijn. The jij-inversion rule (bent → ben) applies to every verb and only to jij/je.

points.title: Hebben — present (heb, hebt, heeft)
points.function: Say what you have
points.explanation: hebben (to have): ik heb, jij hebt (heb jij?), u hebt/heeft, hij/zij heeft, wij/jullie/zij hebben. It is also the engine of the perfect tense later, so these forms pay double.

points.title: Present tense: stem, stem+t, infinitive
points.function: Conjugate any regular verb with three forms
points.explanation: Regular present: ik = the stem (werk), jij/u/hij/zij = stem+t (werkt), all plurals = the infinitive (werken). Inversion drops the -t for jij only: werk jij? Spelling keeps vowel length: schrijven → ik schrijf; komen → ik kom, hij komt.

drills.hint: write — stem, long vowel spelled short

points.title: Plurals: -en and -s
points.function: More than one — and every plural is a de-word
points.explanation: Most nouns take -en (boek → boeken, huis → huizen — note s→z, f→v voicing). Nouns ending in unstressed -e, -el, -er, -en and most loanwords take -s (tafel → tafels, auto → auto's — apostrophe after a long vowel letter). All plurals use de, even het-words: het huis → de huizen.

drills.hint: plural of huis — s becomes z

drills.sentence: Er staan vier {{answer}} in de kamer.
drills.translation: There are four tables in the room.
Two cars are parked at the door.
All the shops are open today.

points.title: Negation: niet and geen
points.function: Two words for 'not' — and the choice is grammatical
points.explanation: geen negates a noun with een or no article: Ik heb geen auto (I have no car), Ik drink geen koffie. niet negates everything else — verbs, adjectives, definite nouns — and gravitates toward the end: Ik werk vandaag niet, De auto is niet duur. If you could say 'not a/no X', it's geen; otherwise niet.

points.title: Questions: inversion and question words
points.function: Ask by flipping — no 'do' needed
points.explanation: Yes/no questions just invert verb and subject: Jij werkt → Werk jij? (remember: no -t before jij). Question words come first, verb second: Waar woon je? (where do you live), Wat doe je? Wie (who), wat (what), waar (where), wanneer (when), waarom (why), hoe (how), welke/welk (which).

points.title: Adjectives: the -e rule
points.function: One little e, one famous exception
points.explanation: Before a noun, adjectives take -e: de grote man, het grote huis, de grote huizen. THE exception: een + het-word + singular = no -e: een groot huis (but: een grote man, because man is a de-word). After the noun, never inflected: het huis is groot.

drills.translation: The white house is for sale.
I am looking for a big house.

points.title: Possessives: mijn, jouw, zijn, haar…
points.function: Say whose it is
points.explanation: mijn (my), jouw/je (your — informal), uw (your — polite), zijn (his), haar (her), ons/onze (our — ons before het-words singular, onze elsewhere!), jullie (your pl.), hun (their). Only ons/onze changes form.

drills.hint: our — het-word singular takes the short form
our — de-word takes the long form

points.title: Er is / er zijn
points.function: There is, there are
points.explanation: Existence uses er: Er is een probleem (there is a problem), Er zijn veel mensen (there are many people). Questions: Is er koffie? Zijn er vragen? This little er returns at B1 with four more jobs — meet it properly now.

drills.translation: There are many people in the street.

drills.sentence: Er {{answer}} geen melk in de koelkast.
drills.translation: There is no milk in the fridge.

drills.sentence: Er {{answer}} twee banken in het park.
drills.translation: There are two benches in the park. ('stand')
drills.hint: Dutch prefers a posture verb here

points.title: The perfect: heb gewerkt, ben gegaan
points.function: The everyday past — Dutch talks about yesterday in the perfect
points.explanation: Spoken Dutch uses the perfect for most past events: hebben/zijn + participle at the END. Regular participles: ge + stem + d/t — t after the 't kofschip consonants (t k f s ch p): gewerkt, but gespeeld. zijn goes with movement/change: Ik ben naar huis gegaan. Common irregulars: gedaan, gezien, geweest, gekocht.

points.title: Imperfect: werkte, speelde
points.function: The story-telling past
points.explanation: The imperfect (simple past) carries stories and descriptions: stem + de(n)/te(n) — te after 't kofschip: werkte, maar speelde. Irregular verbs change vowel: gaan → ging, komen → kwam, zijn → was/waren, hebben → had/hadden. Rule of thumb: single events in speech → perfect; background and narration → imperfect.

drills.translation: I used to live in Leiden.

points.title: Modal verbs: kunnen, moeten, mogen, willen
points.function: Can, must, may, want — with the infinitive at the end
points.explanation: Modals conjugate irregularly and send the main verb (infinitive) to the END: Ik kan morgen komen. kunnen (can): ik kan, hij kan, wij kunnen. moeten (must). mogen (may): ik mag. willen (want): ik wil. zullen (shall/will) drives the future and polite offers: Zal ik helpen?

drills.translation: She wants to become a doctor.

points.title: Separable verbs: opstaan → ik sta op
points.function: The prefix breaks off and lands at the end
points.explanation: Verbs like opstaan (get up), meekomen (come along), aankomen (arrive) split in main clauses: Ik sta om zeven uur op — the prefix flies to the end. In the perfect, ge- slots INSIDE: opgestaan. With modals they stay whole: Ik moet vroeg opstaan.

drills.sentence: De trein komt om tien uur {{answer}}.

drills.translation: I have to get up early tomorrow.

points.title: Subclauses: the verb walks to the end
points.function: After omdat, dat, als — everything changes
points.explanation: In a subclause the conjugated verb moves to the END: Ik blijf thuis omdat ik ziek ben (…because I sick am). Same after dat (that), als (if/when), terwijl (while), toen (when-past). This end-verb rule plus V2 in main clauses IS Dutch word order.

drills.sentence: Ik blijf thuis omdat ik ziek {{answer}}.
drills.translation: I'm staying home because I am sick.
He says that he is coming tomorrow.
If it rains, we stay inside.
I know that she works hard.
While I cook, I listen to music.

drills.sentence: Toen ik jong {{answer}}, woonde ik hier.
drills.translation: When I was young, I lived here.

points.title: Future: gaan + infinitive, zullen
points.function: Plans with gaan, promises with zullen
points.explanation: Everyday future = gaan + infinitive at the end: Ik ga morgen werken. zullen adds promise/prediction/proposal: Ik zal het doen (I'll do it), Het zal regenen. And Dutch loves plain present + time word: Ik kom morgen — perfectly future.

drills.translation: I am going to do groceries tomorrow.
What are you all going to do this weekend?
It is going to snow tonight.
She is going to move next year.

points.title: Comparative and superlative: -er, -st
points.function: Bigger and biggest — with dan, not als
points.explanation: groot → groter → grootst. -r words insert d: duur → duurder. Compare with dan: groter dan ik (bigger than me) — colloquial 'groter als' is widely heard, widely corrected. Irregulars: goed → beter → best, veel → meer → meest, graag → liever → liefst.

drills.sentence: Mijn huis is {{answer}} dan jouw huis.
drills.translation: My house is bigger than yours.
This coat is more expensive than that one.

drills.sentence: Dit is het {{answer}} gebouw van de stad.
drills.translation: This is the tallest building in the city.

drills.sentence: Haar Nederlands is {{answer}} dan het mijne.
drills.translation: Her Dutch is better than mine.
I prefer tea to coffee. ('more gladly')

drills.sentence: Dat is het {{answer}} idee tot nu toe.
drills.translation: That is the best idea so far.

points.title: Diminutives: -je, -tje, -pje
points.function: The most Dutch thing there is
points.explanation: Dutch shrinks everything: huis → huisje, boom → boompje, tafel → tafeltje. The ending depends on the final sound (-je, -tje, -etje, -pje, -kje) — and EVERY diminutive becomes a het-word: het meisje (the girl — literally 'little maid'). Diminutives soften, endear, and sometimes change meaning: biertje (a beer, the normal way to order one).

drills.translation: We live in a little house.
The girl plays in the garden.

drills.sentence: Er zit een {{answer}} in de boom.
drills.translation: There is a little bird in the tree.
Shall we take a little walk?
drills.hint: om + etje — a stroll around the block

points.title: Om te + infinitive
points.function: In order to — purpose and after adjectives
points.explanation: Purpose: Ik ga naar de winkel om brood te kopen (to buy bread) — om opens, te + infinitive closes. After adjectives: Het is moeilijk om Nederlands te leren. With separable verbs, te goes inside: om op te staan.

drills.sentence: Ik ga naar de markt om groente {{answer}} kopen.
drills.translation: I'm going to the market to buy vegetables.
drills.hint: the little word before the infinitive

drills.sentence: Het is leuk {{answer}} nieuwe mensen te ontmoeten.
drills.translation: It is nice to meet new people.

drills.sentence: Zij spaart om een huis te {{answer}}.
drills.translation: She is saving to buy a house.

drills.sentence: Het is tijd om op te {{answer}}.
drills.translation: It is time to get up.
He is calling to ask something.

drills.sentence: Het is moeilijk om vroeg {{answer}} beginnen.
drills.translation: It is hard to start early.

points.title: The imperative
points.function: Tell people what to do — with the bare stem
points.explanation: The imperative is just the stem: Kom hier! Kijk uit! (watch out). Polite version adds u and -t: Komt u binnen (do come in). Softeners make it friendly: Kom maar, Ga even zitten, Doe eens rustig — maar/even/eens turn commands into invitations.

drills.hint: stem of gaan, softened with even
stem of aantrekken — prefix at the end

points.title: Er — the five jobs of one little word
points.function: Location, existence, quantity, prepositions, passive — er does it all
points.explanation: Beyond er is/er zijn: (1) place — Ik woon er al jaren (I've lived THERE for years). (2) quantity — Hoeveel kinderen heb je? Ik heb er drie (I have three OF THEM). (3) preposition fusion — over het boek → erover (about it): Ik denk erover na. (4) passive subject-filler — Er wordt gebeld (someone's ringing). Dutch speakers sprinkle er everywhere; learners leave it out and sound abrupt.

drills.sentence: Hoeveel katten heb je? — Ik heb {{answer}} twee.
drills.translation: How many cats do you have? — I have two (of them).
I have lived there for ten years.

drills.sentence: Denk je nog over het aanbod na? — Ik denk {{answer}} over na.
drills.translation: Are you still considering the offer? — I'm thinking about it.
Someone is knocking on the door.

drills.sentence: Zijn er nog kaartjes? — Ja, {{answer}} zijn er nog vijf.
drills.translation: Are there tickets left? — Yes, there are five left.

points.title: Relative clauses: die, dat, waar
points.function: Who, which, that — chosen by the noun's gender
points.explanation: de-words take die: de man die daar loopt. het-words take dat: het boek dat ik lees. After a preposition, people take wie (de man met wie ik werk) and things take waar+preposition fused: het huis waarin ik woon / waar ik in woon. The verb goes to the end — it's a subclause.

drills.sentence: De vrouw {{answer}} daar staat, is mijn buurvrouw.
drills.translation: The woman who is standing there is my neighbour.

drills.sentence: De fiets {{answer}} ik kocht, is al kapot.
drills.translation: The bicycle I bought is already broken.

drills.sentence: Het boek {{answer}} ik lees, is spannend.
drills.translation: The book I am reading is exciting.

drills.sentence: Het huis {{answer}} te koop staat, is oud.
drills.translation: The house that is for sale is old.

drills.sentence: De collega met {{answer}} ik samenwerk, is aardig.
drills.translation: The colleague I work with is nice.
Do you know who she is talking to?

points.title: Reflexive verbs: zich vergissen, zich voelen
points.function: Verbs that point back at their subject
points.explanation: me/mij (ik), je (jij), zich (hij/zij/u/zij-plural), ons (wij), je (jullie): Ik vergis me (I'm mistaken), Hij voelt zich goed, Wij herinneren ons die dag. Some verbs are always reflexive in Dutch though never in English: zich vervelen (be bored), zich haasten (hurry).

drills.sentence: Wij herinneren {{answer}} die zomer nog goed.
drills.translation: We still remember that summer well.

points.title: The passive: worden and zijn
points.function: It is being done vs it is done
points.explanation: Ongoing passive = worden + participle: De brug wordt gerepareerd (is being repaired). Result state = zijn: De brug is gerepareerd (has been / is repaired). Past: werd gerepareerd. The doer takes door: door de gemeente (by the city).

drills.translation: The road will be repaired next week.
The letter will be sent tomorrow.

drills.sentence: Het boek werd {{answer}} veel mensen gelezen.
drills.translation: The book was read by many people.
A lot is being built here.

points.title: Want vs omdat, dus and maar
points.function: Two 'becauses' with two word orders
points.explanation: want (for/because) is a COORDINATOR — normal V2 order follows: Ik blijf thuis, want ik ben ziek. omdat is a SUBORDINATOR — verb to the end: …omdat ik ziek ben. Same meaning, different grammar. dus (so) and maar (but) also keep main-clause order. Pick your 'because' by the word order you want.

drills.sentence: Ik blijf thuis, {{answer}} ik ben ziek.
drills.translation: I'm staying home, for I am sick.
drills.hint: the because that keeps normal order

drills.sentence: Zij is blij, {{answer}} het is vrijdag.
drills.translation: She is happy, because it is Friday.

drills.sentence: Ik blijf thuis {{answer}} ik ziek ben.
drills.translation: I'm staying home because I am sick.
drills.hint: the because that kicks the verb to the end

drills.sentence: Hij leert Nederlands {{answer}} hij in Utrecht woont.
drills.translation: He is learning Dutch because he lives in Utrecht.
It's raining, so we're staying inside.

drills.sentence: Ik wil komen, {{answer}} ik heb geen tijd.
drills.translation: I want to come, but I have no time.

points.title: Te + infinitive: proberen te, hoeven te
points.function: Which verbs take te — and which don't
points.explanation: Modals and laten/gaan/komen/zien/horen take a bare infinitive: Ik kan zwemmen, Ik laat mijn fiets repareren. Most other verbs need te: Ik probeer te slapen, Je hoeft niet te komen (hoeven = need not, only in negation!), Hij belooft te helpen. Vergeet niet te bellen!

drills.translation: I try to exercise every day.
Don't forget to call your mother!
I am having my hair cut.
I see him cycling every day.
She promises to be on time.

points.title: Zou: would, could you, apparently
points.function: Politeness, hypotheticals, and hearsay in one verb
points.explanation: zou/zouden (past of zullen) covers: polite requests — Zou je me kunnen helpen? (could you help me); hypotheticals — Ik zou een huis kopen (I would buy a house); advice — Je zou meer moeten slapen (you should sleep more); hearsay — Hij zou rijk zijn (he is said to be rich).

drills.sentence: Ik {{answer}} graag een huis met een tuin willen.
drills.translation: I would like a house with a garden.

drills.sentence: Wat {{answer}} jij doen in mijn plaats?
drills.translation: What would you do in my place?
He is said to be a millionaire.

points.title: Indirect speech: dat and of
points.function: Report statements with dat, questions with of
points.explanation: Statements: Hij zegt dat hij moe is (verb to the end). Yes/no questions: Ze vraagt of ik kom (of = whether). Question-word questions keep their word: Hij vraagt waar ik woon. All are subclauses — verb final, every time.

drills.translation: He says that he is coming tomorrow.
She asks whether I have time.

drills.sentence: Ik weet niet {{answer}} hij thuis is.
drills.translation: I don't know whether he is home.
She asks when the shop opens.

drills.sentence: Hij vertelt dat hij in Gent {{answer}}.
drills.translation: He says that he lives in Ghent.
Do you know whether she is coming?

points.title: Modal particles: wel, toch, maar, even, eens
points.function: The seasoning that makes Dutch sound Dutch
points.explanation: Untranslatable but essential: wel contradicts softly (Het is wel lekker — it IS nice, actually); toch seeks agreement or marks 'after all' (Je komt toch?); maar softens commands (Ga maar zitten); even makes things quick and casual (Ik bel je even); eens adds 'just/sometime' (Kom eens kijken!). Stack them: Kom maar eens even kijken.

drills.translation: I'll give you a quick call.
It's not as bad as it seems.

points.title: Past perfect and tense sequencing
points.function: What had happened before something else happened
points.explanation: had/waren + participle: Ik had al gegeten toen zij kwam (I had already eaten when she came). Movement/change verbs take waren: Zij waren al vertrokken. In stories the past perfect sets the pre-history, the imperfect carries the narrative, and toen (when — one past moment) pairs with it constantly.

drills.sentence: Ik {{answer}} al gegeten toen zij kwam.
drills.translation: I had already eaten when she came.
We had already sold the house.

drills.sentence: Toen ik aankwam, {{answer}} het feest al begonnen.
drills.translation: When I arrived, the party had already started.

drills.sentence: Zij had de brief nog niet {{answer}}.
drills.translation: She had not yet read the letter.

points.title: Double infinitives: heb kunnen doen
points.function: Perfect + modal = two infinitives at the end
points.explanation: When a modal goes into the perfect, both verbs stand as infinitives: Ik heb niet kunnen slapen (NOT gekund!), Zij heeft willen komen, Wij hadden moeten bellen. Order: auxiliary … modal-infinitive + main-infinitive. Same with laten and zien: Ik heb mijn fiets laten repareren.

points.title: Progressive: aan het + infinitive, zitten te
points.function: Right now, in the middle of it
points.explanation: Dutch marks 'busy doing' with aan het + infinitive: Ik ben aan het koken (I'm cooking). The posture verbs add flavour AND te: Hij zit te lezen (he sits reading), Zij staat te wachten, Ze liggen te slapen. Pick the posture that's literally true — Dutch does.

drills.translation: He has been sitting reading for hours.

drills.sentence: Ik was net aan het {{answer}} toen je belde.
drills.translation: I was just eating when you called.

points.title: Waar-compounds: waarover, erop, daarmee
points.function: Preposition + it/what/that fuse into one word
points.explanation: Prepositions never combine with het/dat/wat directly — they fuse: over het → erover, over dat → daarover, over wat → waarover. Waar denk je aan? (what are you thinking about — splittable!), Ik ben het ermee eens (I agree with it), Daar heb ik geen zin in. met becomes -mee, tot becomes -toe.

drills.sentence: Ik ben het er niet {{answer}} eens.

drills.hint: the preposition lands at the end

points.title: Conditionals: als … zou, had gedaan
points.function: If-worlds, real and missed
points.explanation: Real: Als het regent, blijven we thuis. Hypothetical: Als ik rijk was/zou zijn, zou ik reizen (if I were rich I would travel). Missed past: Als ik het had geweten, was ik gekomen / zou ik gekomen zijn (if I had known…). The als-clause is verb-final; the main clause often starts with dan.

drills.sentence: Als ik rijk was, {{answer}} ik de wereld rondreizen.
drills.translation: If I were rich, I would travel the world.

drills.sentence: Als het morgen {{answer}}, gaan we naar het strand.
drills.translation: If it is sunny tomorrow, we'll go to the beach.

drills.sentence: Als ik het {{answer}} geweten, was ik gekomen.
drills.translation: If I had known, I would have come.

drills.sentence: Wat zou je doen als je de loterij {{answer}} winnen?
drills.translation: What would you do if you were to win the lottery?

drills.sentence: Als je harder had gewerkt, {{answer}} je geslaagd.
drills.translation: If you had worked harder, you would have passed.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} het regent, dan blijven we binnen.
drills.translation: If it rains, then we stay inside.

points.title: Register: u-space, je-space, and writing
points.function: Choose the right distance
points.explanation: The Netherlands went largely informal — colleagues, shops, and ads say je; u survives for elders, officials, first contacts, and formal letters (Geachte heer/mevrouw … Met vriendelijke groet). Flanders keeps u warmer and longer. Switching too early ('tutoyeren') can sting; offering the switch is a small ritual: Zeg maar je, hoor.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} heer De Vries, — zo opent een formele brief.
drills.translation: Dear Mr De Vries, — the formal letter opening.
With kind regards — the standard sign-off.

drills.sentence: Tegen een oudere klant zeg je {{answer}}, geen je.
drills.translation: To an older customer you say u, not je.
Feel free to use the informal you.
In Flanders, u stays customary longer.
Among colleagues, informal you is normal nowadays.

points.title: Nominalization: het + infinitive, -heid, ge-
points.function: Turn actions into things
points.explanation: Any infinitive nominalizes with het: het reizen (travelling), het leren van een taal. -heid builds qualities: vrijheid (freedom), snelheid. ge- + stem makes (often annoyed) activity nouns: dat gepraat (all that talking), het geloop. Formal prose leans on these; conversation prefers verbs.

drills.translation: All that talking is driving me mad!

drills.sentence: De snel{{answer}} van het antwoord verbaasde me.
drills.translation: The speed of the reply surprised me.

drills.sentence: Dat eeuwige {{answer}} in de gangen stoort iedereen.
drills.translation: That endless walking about in the corridors bothers everyone.

points.title: The tang: brackets around the sentence
points.function: Dutch sentences close like pincers — master the frame
points.explanation: The conjugated verb (position 2) and everything verbal at the end form a 'tang' (pincer) that brackets the middle: Ik heb gisteren met mijn moeder over de vakantie gebeld. Long middles are fine; breaking the bracket is not. Exception — extraposition: prepositional phrases may follow the end-verb (…gebeld over de vakantie), which relaxed prose uses for breath.

drills.sentence: Ik heb gisteren de hele dag aan mijn scriptie {{answer}}.
drills.translation: I worked on my thesis all day yesterday.

drills.sentence: Zij wil volgend jaar in Amsterdam gaan {{answer}}.
drills.translation: She wants to go study in Amsterdam next year.

drills.sentence: Hij heeft ons gisteren pas over het plan {{answer}}.
drills.translation: He only told us about the plan yesterday.

drills.sentence: Ik moet morgen vroeg naar het station {{answer}}.
drills.translation: I have to leave for the station early tomorrow.

drills.sentence: Wij hebben lang op de uitslag {{answer}}.
drills.translation: We waited a long time for the result.

drills.sentence: Ze heeft de kinderen naar school {{answer}}.
drills.translation: She took the children to school.

points.title: Posture verbs as 'to be': staan, liggen, zitten
points.function: Where English says 'is', Dutch says stands, lies, or sits
points.explanation: Dutch locates things by posture: De fles staat op tafel (bottle stands — upright), Het boek ligt op tafel (lies — flat), De sleutels zitten in mijn zak (sit — contained). Hangen for hanging things, lopen for ongoing processes (Het loopt goed). Using zijn instead is grammatical but instantly foreign.

drills.translation: The bottle is (standing) on the table.
The vase is on the cupboard.
The newspaper is (lying) on the doormat.
The keys are in my coat pocket.

drills.sentence: Er {{answer}} een gat in mijn sok.
drills.translation: There is a hole in my sock.

points.title: Netherlands vs Flanders: one language, two flavours
points.function: Read both sides of the border
points.explanation: Standard Dutch is shared; flavour differs. Belgian Dutch keeps ge/gij (you) colloquially, prefers u warmth, and has its own lexicon: goesting (zin — desire), plezant (leuk), hesp (ham), proper (schoon — clean); 'schoon' itself means BEAUTIFUL in Flanders. Pronunciation is softer (the 'zachte g'). Official register ('VRT-Nederlands') aligns with the north, but tussentaal — the in-between — rules Flemish daily life.

drills.sentence: Vlaams voor 'zin': Ik heb {{answer}} in frieten.

drills.translation: Flemish informal: What are you doing there?
drills.hint: the question word — note zijt gij

drills.sentence: 'Dat is {{answer}}!' — zegt een Vlaming over iets moois.
drills.translation: 'That is beautiful!' — as a Fleming says it.
drills.hint: means clean up north, beautiful down south

drills.translation: Flemish for 'ham': a sandwich with ham.

drills.sentence: De zachte {{answer}} kleurt het zuidelijke accent.
drills.translation: The 'soft g' colours the southern accent.

points.title: Fossils: der, des, te allen tijde
points.function: Read the case endings Dutch officially buried
points.explanation: Dutch dropped its case system, but fossils walk the streets: genitives — de heer des huizes (the master of the house), de loop der dingen (the course of things), 's ochtends (= des ochtends, in the morning); datives in fixed phrases — te allen tijde (at all times), ten slotte, ter plaatse. Productive only in high style and irony; obligatory in reading.

drills.sentence: De loop {{answer}} dingen — zo gaat het leven.
drills.translation: The course of things — such is life.
In the morning I drink coffee.
Finally, I want to thank you.

drills.sentence: De heer {{answer}} huizes opent de deur.
drills.translation: The master of the house opens the door.
